backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1171:      "response_text": "Según los fragmentos proporcionados, los ingresos totales de NVIDIA en el año fiscal 2024 fueron de **$60,922 millones** (es decir, $60.9 mil millones) [2]. Esto representa un aumento del **126%** en comparación con el año fiscal 2023, cuando los ingresos fueron de $26,974 millones [2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1308:      "response_text": "Según el fragmento [4], el modelo Transformer base emplea **h = 8** cabezas de atención paralelas (attention heads).",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1415:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, here are the hyperparameters for the Transformer base model and big model:\n\n| Hyperparameter | Base Model | Big Model |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Dropout rate (Pdrop)** | 0.1 [1][2] | 0.1 [1] |\n| **Checkpoint averaging** | Last 5 checkpoints (written at 10-minute intervals) [1] | Last 20 checkpoints [1] |\n| **Beam size** | 4 [1] | 4 [1] |\n| **Length penalty (α)** | 0.6 [1] | 0.6 [1] |\n| **Maximum output length** | Input length + 50 (terminate early when possible) [1] | Input length + 50 (terminate early when possible) [1] |\n| **Label smoothing (ϵls)** | 0.1 [2] | Not explicitly stated for big model, but likely same as base model |\n| **Warmup steps** | 4000 [2] | Not explicitly stated for big model, but likely same as base model |\n| **Learning rate schedule** | Linear increase for first warmup_steps, then decrease proportionally to inverse square root of step number [2] | Same as base model (implied) |\n\n**Additional notes from the fragments:**\n- The big model used a dropout rate of 0.1 instead of 0.3 [1], suggesting the base model may have used 0.3 in some contexts, but the fragments state the base model used Pdrop = 0.1 [2].\n- For the big model, the fragments mention: `big 6 1024 4096 0.1 300K` [3], which likely corresponds to: number of layers = 6, model dimension = 1024, feed-forward dimension = 4096, dropout = 0.1, and training steps = 300K.\n- The base model is described as having 27.3 BLEU on EN-DE and 38.1 BLEU on EN-FR, while the big model achieved 28.4 BLEU on EN-DE and 41.8 BLEU on EN-FR [2][4].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1454:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, here is NVIDIA's segment revenue breakdown for the fiscal years ended January 28, 2024, and January 29, 2023:\n\n| Reportable Segment | Fiscal Year 2024 ($M) | Fiscal Year 2023 ($M) | $ Change | % Change |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Compute & Networking** | $47,405 | $15,068 | $32,337 | 215% |\n| **Graphics** | $13,517 | $11,906 | $1,611 | 14% |\n| **Total Revenue** | **$60,922** | **$26,974** | **$33,948** | **126%** |\n\n[1]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1489:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, here are the key financial metrics for NVIDIA in FY2024 (year ended January 28, 2024):\n\n- **Revenue:** $60,922 million (up 126% from the prior year) [2]\n- **Net Income:** $29,760 million (up 581% from the prior year) [2]\n- **Gross Margin:** 72.7% (up 15.8 percentage points from the prior year) [2]\n\nAdditional details from the fragments include:\n- **Operating Income:** $32,972 million (up 681%) [2]\n- **Net Income Per Diluted Share:** $11.93 (up 586%) [2]\n- **Revenue by Segment:**\n  - **Compute & Networking:** $47,405 million (up 215%) [1]\n  - **Graphics:** $13,517 million (up 14%) [1]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1525:      "response_text": "I can only answer questions about the provided document(s). Would you like to ask about its content? For example, I can help with questions about the Compensation Recovery Policy, officer obligations, or other topics covered in the fragments.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1559:      "response_text": "I cannot confirm that finding. In fact, the document fragments state the **opposite**. The paper presents the Transformer as a **new simple network architecture** that is **superior** to recurrent models in several ways:\n\n- The Transformer is **based solely on attention mechanisms**, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely [1].\n- Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be **superior in quality** while being **more parallelizable** and requiring **significantly less time to train** [1].\n- The Transformer achieves **28.4 BLEU** on WMT 2014 English-to-German translation, improving over existing best results by **over 2 BLEU**, and establishes a **new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8** on English-to-French [1].\n- For translation tasks, the Transformer can be trained **significantly faster** than architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers [3].\n- The paper notes that recurrent models' **inherently sequential nature precludes parallelization** within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths [2].\n\nThe fragments do **not** contain any statement claiming RNNs are superior to Transformers.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1598:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, **NVIDIA's revenue did NOT decline by 50% in fiscal year 2024.** In fact, the data shows a significant increase.\n\nHere are the key facts from the documents:\n\n*   **Revenue Growth:** Revenue for fiscal year 2024 was **$60.9 billion**, up **126%** from $26.974 billion in fiscal year 2023 [2].\n*   **Net Income Growth:** Net income rose **581%** to $29.76 billion [2].\n*   **Segment Performance:**\n    *   **Compute & Networking** revenue increased by **215%** to $47.4 billion [1].\n    *   **Graphics** revenue increased by **14%** to $13.5 billion [1].\n*   **Data Center Growth:** Data Center revenue was up **217%** for the fiscal year, driven by strong demand from enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and multiple industry verticals [2].\n\n**Conclusion:** The statement that NVIDIA's revenue declined by 50% in 2024 is incorrect based on the document. The actual result was a **126% increase** in revenue. The documents do not mention any decline.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1633:        "fragments"
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1669:      "response_text": "Based solely on the provided document fragments, the Transformer model has the following advantages:\n\n- **Superior in quality** on machine translation tasks, achieving **28.4 BLEU** on WMT 2014 English-to-German (improving over existing best results by over 2 BLEU) and **41.8 BLEU** on WMT 2014 English-to-French (a new single-model state-of-the-art) [1].\n- **More parallelizable** than recurrent or convolutional models, as it dispenses with recurrence and convolutions entirely [1].\n- **Requires significantly less time to train** compared to architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers [1][6].\n- **Lower training cost** (measured in FLOPs) than previous state-of-the-art models, as shown in Table 2 [6].\n- **Outperforms all previously reported models** on English constituency parsing (except the Recurrent Neural Network Grammar), even without task-specific tuning [5].\n- **Outperforms the BerkeleyParser** on English constituency parsing even when training only on the WSJ training set of 40K sentences [5].\n- **Can be trained significantly faster** than architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers for translation tasks [7].\n- **Achieves new state of the art** on both WMT 2014 English-to-German and English-to-French translation tasks [7].\n- **Outperforms even all previously reported ensembles** on the English-to-German translation task [7].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1708:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, NVIDIA mentions the following product categories and platforms:\n\n**Compute & Networking Segment**\n- **Data Center accelerated computing platforms** [1]\n- **End-to-end networking platforms**: Quantum for InfiniBand and Spectrum for Ethernet [1]\n- **NVIDIA DRIVE** (automated-driving platform) [1]\n- **DGX Cloud** (AI-training-as-a-service platform) [3][5]\n- **NeMo** (solution for building enterprise-ready Large Language Models) [3]\n- **NVIDIA AI Enterprise software** [5]\n- **Acceleration libraries, APIs, SDKs, and domain-specific application frameworks** [5]\n- **Networking offerings**: network adapters, cables, DPUs, switch systems, and a full software stack for InfiniBand and Ethernet [5]\n- **Compute solutions**: GPUs, CPUs, and DPUs [3]\n\n**Graphics Segment**\n- **GeForce RTX and GeForce GTX GPUs** for gaming desktop and laptop PCs [6]\n- **NVIDIA RTX** (platform featuring ray tracing technology and deep learning super sampling / NVIDIA DLSS) [6]\n- **GeForce NOW** (cloud gaming service for playing PC games on underpowered devices) [6]\n- **SoCs and development platforms** for gaming [6]\n- **Workstation-class GPUs** for professional artists, architects, and designers [1]\n- **PC GPUs** for gamers, live streamers, broadcasters, artists, creators, and generative AI applications [1]\n\n**Other Platforms Mentioned**\n- **NVIDIA AI supercomputers** [3]\n- **CUDA and other software tools** (used by over 4.7 million developers worldwide) [3]\n- **DGX** (AI supercomputers) [3]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:1747:      "response_text": "Based solely on the provided document fragments, the following exceptions and carve-outs are mentioned:\n\n1.  **Right to Copy:** There is an exception to the confidentiality obligations allowing a party to copy the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party **solely for the Purpose** [1].\n2.  **Disclosure to Employees and Professional Advisers:** The Recipient is permitted to disclose Confidential Information to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know it for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence, and who are bound by obligations [2].\n3.  **Duration of Undertakings:** The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 (regarding use and security of information) will continue in force either **indefinitely** or **for a specified number of years** from the date of the Agreement [1]. This provides an optional carve-out from an indefinite term.\n4.  **Jurisdiction:** The English Courts have **non-exclusive** jurisdiction to deal with any dispute [1]. This is a carve-out from exclusive jurisdiction, allowing disputes to potentially be heard in other courts.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2015:      "response_text": "The provided document fragments do not contain the total number of NVIDIA employees at the end of fiscal year 2024. \n\nWhile the fragments include workforce diversity statistics (79% male, 20% female, 1% not declared, with 6% of U.S. workforce composed of Black/African American and Hispanic/Latino employees) [6], the actual total headcount number is not mentioned in any of the fragments.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2048:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the dimensionality of the model (d_model) in the Transformer base model is **512**.\n\nThis is stated in the description of the Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks: \"The dimensionality of input and output is dmodel = 512, and the inner-layer has dimensionality dff = 2048.\" [8]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2082:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, the Transformer base model uses **8 attention heads**.\n\n- The document states: \"In this work we employ h = 8 parallel attention layers, or heads.\" [2]\n- This is further confirmed in the model variations section, where the base model configuration is listed with **h=8** in the table showing variations on the Transformer architecture [7].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2118:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, the duration or term of the confidentiality obligations is addressed in clause 9:\n\n- The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 (which cover non-use and non-disclosure of Confidential Information) will continue in force **either indefinitely or for a specified number of years** from the date of the Agreement [2].\n\nThe document provides two options for the parties to choose from:\n- **[Indefinitely]** [2]\n- **[For [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement]** [2]\n\nThe specific duration is not filled in within these fragments, as the placeholder text indicates it is to be determined and inserted by the parties.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2153:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, **no specific types of information are explicitly excluded** from the definition of Confidential Information.\n\nThe fragments only state that the parties intend to disclose \"information (the Confidential Information)\" for a specified Purpose [1]. The document does not contain any clauses listing categories of information that would *not* be considered confidential (such as publicly available information, independently developed information, or information received from a third party).\n\nThis specific information is not available in the provided document fragments.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2188:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is the comparison of NVIDIA's Data Center and Gaming segment revenue for fiscal year 2024 (ended January 28, 2024):\n\n*   **Data Center revenue**: $47,525 million [4]\n*   **Gaming revenue**: $10,447 million [4]\n\n**Key Details:**\n*   Data Center revenue was significantly higher than Gaming revenue in fiscal year 2024.\n*   The year-on-year increase for Data Center revenue was 217% [1]. In contrast, Gaming revenue was up 15% from fiscal year 2023 [6].\n*   Data Center compute revenue specifically was up 244% in the fiscal year [1].\n*   Strong demand in the Data Center segment was driven by enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and multiple industry verticals including automotive, financial services, and healthcare [1].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2224:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, NVIDIA's gross margin increased significantly from fiscal year 2023 to fiscal year 2024.\n\n-   In fiscal year 2023, the gross margin was **56.9%** [2].\n-   In fiscal year 2024, the gross margin was **72.7%** [2].\n\nThis represents an increase of **15.8 percentage points** [2]. The gross profit as a percentage of revenue also reflects this change, moving from 56.9% in fiscal year 2023 to 72.7% in fiscal year 2024 [1].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2260:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, a direct comparison of NVIDIA's research and development (R&D) expenses in absolute dollar amounts between fiscal years 2023 and 2024 is not available. However, the fragments do provide a comparison as a percentage of revenue.\n\n*   In fiscal year 2024, R&D expenses were **14.2%** of revenue [1].\n*   In fiscal year 2023, R&D expenses were **27.2%** of revenue [1].\n\nThis shows that while R&D spending as a percentage of total revenue decreased significantly, total operating expenses remained relatively flat. The document notes that total operating expenses were **$11,329 million** in fiscal year 2024, up only 2% from **$11,132 million** in fiscal year 2023 [2]. This small increase in total operating expenses, which includes R&D, occurred while revenue increased by 126% [2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2297:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is the comparison of BLEU scores for the Transformer base and big models on English-to-German translation:\n\n*   **Transformer (base model)**: 27.3 BLEU [1]\n*   **Transformer (big)**: 28.4 BLEU [1]\n\nThe document notes that the Transformer (big) model achieved a new state-of-the-art score, improving over existing best results, including ensembles, by over 2 BLEU [4].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2333:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the training cost of the Transformer is significantly lower than that of other models mentioned, while achieving better or comparable performance.\n\nHere are the specific comparisons detailed in the fragments:\n\n*   **Overall Comparison**: The Transformer achieves better BLEU scores \"at a fraction of the training cost\" compared to previous state-of-the-art models [1].\n*   **English-to-German (EN-DE) Task**:\n    *   The **Transformer (base model)** achieves a BLEU score of 27.3 with a training cost of **3.3 · 10¹⁸** FLOPs [1].\n    *   This cost is drastically lower than models like **GNMT + RL** (2.6 · 10¹⁸ FLOPs for a lower BLEU of 24.6) and **ConvS2S** (9.6 · 10¹⁸ FLOPs for a BLEU of 25.16) [1].\n    *   The **Transformer (big)** model achieves a BLEU of 28.4 with a cost of **2.3 · 10¹⁹** FLOPs, which is still a fraction of the cost of ensemble models like **ConvS2S Ensemble** (3.3 · 10¹⁸ FLOPs is listed for the base model, but the big model's cost is lower than other high-performing ensembles) [1].\n*   **English-to-French (EN-FR) Task**:\n    *   The **Transformer (base model)** achieves a BLEU of 38.1 with a training cost of **1.8 · 10¹⁹** FLOPs [1].\n    *   The **Transformer (big)** model achieves a BLEU of 41.8 with a training cost of **2.0 · 10¹⁹** FLOPs [1].\n    *   For comparison, the **GNMT + RL** model had a training cost of 1.5 · 10²⁰ FLOPs for a lower BLEU of 39.9, and the **ConvS2S** model cost 1.2 · 10²⁰ FLOPs for a BLEU of 40.46 [1].\n\nThe document also notes that the method for estimating training cost involved multiplying the training time, the number of GPUs used, and an estimate of the sustained single-precision floating-point capacity of each GPU [2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2370:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the text primarily outlines the obligations of the **receiving party**. The specific obligations of the **disclosing party** are not detailed in these fragments.\n\nHere is what the document states regarding the receiving party's obligations:\n\n*   **Purpose Limitation:** The Recipient must not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than the defined \"Purpose\" without the other party's written agreement [1].\n*   **Security and Non-Disclosure:** The Recipient must keep the information secure and not disclose it to any third party, with a possible exception for employees and professional advisers who need it for the Purpose and are bound by confidentiality obligations [1].\n*   **Copying Restriction:** The Recipient's right to copy the Confidential Information is limited solely to what is necessary for the Purpose [2].\n*   **Duration:** These undertakings of non-use and non-disclosure continue for a period that is either indefinite or for a specified number of years from the date of the Agreement [2].\n\nThe fragments do not contain any clauses that explicitly describe separate or different obligations for the **disclosing party**. The information available is therefore insufficient to make a comparison between the two parties' duties.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2406:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are the risk factors related to NVIDIA's supply chain:\n\n*   **Supply and Demand Mismatch**: Inaccurate estimates of customer demand can lead to a significant mismatch between supply and demand, resulting in product shortages or excess inventory, which has significantly harmed financial results [4].\n*   **Long-Term Supply Commitments**: The company may enter into long-term supply and capacity commitments but may not be able to secure sufficient capacity to address business needs, or long-term demand expectations may change [4].\n*   **Supply Chain Complexity**: Risks may increase as NVIDIA shortens product development cycles, enters new lines of business, or integrates new suppliers or components, creating additional supply chain complexity [4].\n*   **Third-Party Component Availability**: The ability to sell certain products has been and could be impeded if components necessary for finished products are not available from third parties. This risk may increase due to the company's platform strategy [4].\n*   **Extended Lead Times**: During periods of shortages impacting the semiconductor industry or limited supply/capacity, the lead times on orders may be extended [4].\n*   **Non-Cancellable and Non-Returnable Terms**: The placement of non-cancellable and non-returnable purchasing terms well in advance of historical lead times has amplified negative impacts, such as inventory write-downs and cancellation penalties. This risk has increased and may continue to grow as purchase obligations become a greater portion of total supply [3].\n*   **Pricing Pressures**: Overestimation of future demand has led to reductions in average selling prices, including through channel pricing programs. Additionally, supplier price increases have forced NVIDIA to increase prices for certain products, which may need to continue [3].\n*   **Non-Linear Supply Deliveries**: Supply deliveries and production may be non-linear within a quarter or year [4].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2442:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are the key contributions of the paper:\n\n*   **Proposes a new network architecture called the Transformer**, which is based solely on attention mechanisms and does away with recurrence and convolutions entirely [1].\n*   **Introduces the Scaled Dot-Product Attention mechanism**, which computes the dot products of queries with all keys, scales them, and applies a softmax function to obtain weights for the values [2].\n*   **Introduces Multi-Head Attention**, which linearly projects queries, keys, and values multiple times with different learned projections, allowing the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions [3].\n*   **Demonstrates superior quality and efficiency** on machine translation tasks, achieving state-of-the-art BLEU scores (28.4 on English-to-German and 41.8 on English-to-French) while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train [1].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2479:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the key obligations and restrictions imposed by this Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) are as follows:\n\n*   **Purpose Limitation**: The Recipient must not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than the specified Purpose without first obtaining the other party's written agreement [1].\n*   **Security and Non-Disclosure**: The Recipient must keep the Confidential Information secure and not disclose it to any third party. An exception is made for employees and professional advisers who need to know the information for the Purpose, provided they are aware of and bound by a duty of confidence [1].\n*   **Copying Restriction**: The Recipient has no right to copy the Confidential Information, except solely for the Purpose [2].\n*   **Duration**: The obligations of security and non-disclosure (clauses 3 and 4) will continue in force either indefinitely or for a specified number of years from the date of the Agreement [2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2515:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, **yes, NVIDIA's Data Center segment is growing significantly faster than the Gaming segment.**\n\nHere is the breakdown from the fiscal year 2024 data (year ended January 28, 2024) compared to fiscal year 2023:\n\n*   **Data Center Revenue Growth:** Data Center revenue for fiscal year 2024 was **up 217%** [1]. This growth is reflected in the \"Compute & Networking\" segment, which includes Data Center accelerated computing platforms and saw a revenue increase of **215%** [2].\n*   **Gaming Revenue Growth:** Gaming revenue for fiscal year 2024 was **up 15%** from fiscal year 2023 [7].\n\nThe document explicitly states that the year-on-year increase in Compute & Networking revenue was \"due to higher Data Center revenue\" [2], while Gaming revenue grew at a much more moderate rate [7].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2552:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, I can infer the following about NVIDIA's strategic priorities from their R&D spending patterns:\n\n*   **Prioritization of Compute & Networking over Graphics:** The operating income data shows a massive shift in profitability towards the Compute & Networking segment. In fiscal year 2024, this segment generated **$32,016 million** in operating income, a **530% increase** from the previous year, compared to the Graphics segment's **$5,846 million** (a 28% increase) [2]. This suggests that R&D investment is heavily prioritized toward the data center, AI, and networking platforms that drive this segment's growth.\n\n*   **Heavy Investment in AI and Data Center Platforms:** The document states that strong demand was driven by enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and multiple industry verticals, with Data Center compute revenue up **244%** [3]. The company introduced platforms like **NVIDIA DGX Cloud** and **AI Foundations** for custom AI models, and an estimated **40% of Data Center revenue was for AI inference** [7]. This indicates a strategic priority to capture the AI market through significant R&D in both hardware and software stacks.\n\n*   **Focus on Full-Stack and Software Ecosystem Development:** NVIDIA's strategy involves a \"deep and broad software stack\" that includes the **CUDA programming model**, hundreds of domain-specific software libraries, SDKs, and APIs to accelerate performance and ease deployment [6]. R&D spending is clearly directed at maintaining and expanding this software ecosystem, which creates a competitive moat around its hardware.\n\n*   **Commitment to Energy Efficiency:** A specific R&D goal is improving energy efficiency. The company highlights that GPU-accelerated computing is inherently more energy-efficient and that their products powered **24 of the top 30 most energy-efficient systems** on the Green500 list [4]. They also plan to build **Earth-2**, a digital twin of the Earth for climate change prediction [4]. This shows a strategic priority to lead in energy-efficient computing as a key product feature and for sustainability goals.\n\n*   **Expansion into New Business Models:** The risk factors section explicitly mentions the need to \"launch new offerings with new business models including software, services, and cloud solutions\" [8]. This aligns with the introduction of DGX Cloud and AI Foundations, indicating that R&D is not just for physical products but also for developing and scaling service-based revenue streams.\n\n*   **Relative R&D Spending as a Percentage of Revenue:** While total operating expenses increased only **2%** year-over-year, revenue grew by **126%** [3]. This caused R&D expenses to fall dramatically as a percentage of revenue, from **27.2%** in fiscal 2023 to **14.2%** in fiscal 2024 [2]. This suggests that while R&D investment in absolute dollars likely increased, the explosive revenue growth, particularly from the Compute & Networking segment, has significantly outpaced it, reflecting a period of rapid monetization of prior R&D investments.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2593:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the authors argue that self-attention is better than recurrence for long sequences primarily due to the **inherently sequential nature of recurrent models**, which prevents parallelization and becomes a critical bottleneck.\n\nHere are the specific points found in the fragments:\n\n- **Sequential Computation Prevents Parallelization:** Recurrent models process input sequences step-by-step, generating a hidden state as a function of the previous state and the current input. The document states this \"inherently sequential nature precludes parallelization within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples\" [2].\n\n- **Motivation for Self-Attention:** The authors explicitly state they are motivated to use self-attention by considering desiderata that address the shortcomings of recurrent and convolutional layers. One of these key factors is \"the amount of computation that can be parallelized\" [1].\n\nThe fragments do not contain a direct, explicit statement saying \"self-attention is better than recurrence for long sequences because...\", but the reasoning is clearly laid out by contrasting the parallelizable nature of self-attention with the sequential bottleneck of recurrent models, which is especially problematic for long sequences [1][2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2629:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, I can identify the following limitations of the Transformer architecture:\n\n*   **Performance Degradation with Too Many Attention Heads**: While multi-head attention is beneficial, quality drops off when too many heads are used. In the variations tested, single-head attention was 0.9 BLEU worse than the best setting, but quality also declined with an excessive number of heads [6].\n*   **Sensitivity of the Dot-Product Compatibility Function**: Reducing the attention key size \\(d_k\\) hurts model quality. This suggests that determining compatibility is not easy and that a more sophisticated compatibility function than the simple dot product may be beneficial [6].\n*   **Lack of Task-Specific Tuning for Parsing**: In the English constituency parsing task, the model performed surprisingly well without task-specific tuning, but it was still outperformed by the Recurrent Neural Network Grammar [4]. This indicates a limitation in achieving state-of-the-art results on this specific task without further adaptation.\n*   **Perplexity Hurt by Label Smoothing**: The regularization technique of label smoothing, while improving accuracy, hurts perplexity because the model learns to be more unsure [7].\n\nThe fragments do not contain a dedicated \"Limitations\" section, so this list is derived from experimental observations and architectural discussions.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2666:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, I can only analyze the obligations explicitly stated. The document does not contain a comprehensive risk assessment, but I can outline the burdens placed on each party.\n\nThe document places the **majority of explicit obligations and, therefore, the primary legal risk, on the receiving party (the \"Recipient\")**.\n\nHere is a breakdown of the obligations found in the fragments:\n\n*   **Definition of Recipient**: The agreement defines the party receiving information as \"the Recipient\" [2].\n*   **Use Restriction**: The Recipient is strictly limited in how they can use the information. They cannot use the \"Confidential Information disclosed by the other party for any purpose except the Purpose\" without prior written agreement [2].\n*   **Security and Non-Disclosure**: The Recipient must keep the information \"secure and not to disclose it to any third party\" [2]. The only exception mentioned is for employees and professional advisers who need to know for the Purpose and are bound by confidentiality obligations [2].\n*   **Copying Restriction**: The Recipient's right to copy the information is limited \"solely for the Purpose\" [3].\n*   **Duration**: These restrictive undertakings on the Recipient continue for a period that can be set as \"indefinitely\" or for a specified number of years [3].\n\nThe fragments do not detail any specific risks or obligations for the **disclosing party**, such as a warranty that the information is accurate or non-infringing. The document's focus is entirely on controlling the Recipient's behavior.\n\nTherefore, based on the available text, the **receiving party** bears the more clearly defined and substantial risk of breaching the agreement.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2702:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is an analysis of whether this NDA could be used to protect trade secrets:\n\n**Yes, this NDA could be used to protect trade secrets**, for the following reasons:\n\n- **Broad definition of protected information**: The agreement covers \"Confidential Information\" disclosed for a specific purpose [1]. Trade secrets would naturally fall within the scope of confidential information.\n\n- **Purpose limitation**: The Recipient is restricted from using the disclosed information \"for any purpose except the Purpose\" without written consent [1]. This prevents the recipient from exploiting trade secrets for their own benefit.\n\n- **Security and non-disclosure obligations**: The Recipient must keep the information \"secure\" and not disclose it to third parties, with limited exceptions for employees and professional advisers who need to know it and are bound by confidentiality obligations [1]. This aligns with the reasonable steps required to maintain trade secret protection.\n\n- **Limited copying rights**: The Recipient only has the right to copy the confidential information \"solely for the Purpose\" [2], further restricting misuse of trade secret materials.\n\n- **Duration of obligations**: The confidentiality undertakings can continue \"indefinitely\" [2], which is critical for trade secrets, as their value depends on remaining secret indefinitely (unlike other confidential information that may lose sensitivity over time).\n\n**Potential limitations to note:**\n\n- The document is a template with blank fields (dates, party names, purpose description, duration) [1][2], so its effectiveness would depend on how these are completed.\n- The specific definition of \"Confidential Information\" is not detailed in these fragments, so the agreement's strength would depend on whether trade secrets are explicitly or implicitly covered by the disclosure process described.\n\nIn summary, the core mechanisms of this NDA—purpose limitation, security obligations, non-disclosure, and indefinite duration—are well-suited to protecting trade secrets [1][2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:2738:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the Data Center revenue as a percentage of NVIDIA's total revenue in fiscal year 2024 is not explicitly stated. However, we can calculate it using the available figures:\n\n- **Data Center revenue** for fiscal year 2024 was **$47,525 million** [3].\n- **Total revenue** for fiscal year 2024 was **$60,922 million** [1][2].\n\nCalculating the percentage: ($47,525 / $60,922) × 100 = **approximately 78.0%**.\n\nTherefore, Data Center revenue accounted for roughly **78%** of NVIDIA's total revenue in fiscal year 2024.",

backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3353:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, here are the hyperparameters for the Transformer base and big models:\n\n| Hyperparameter | Base Model | Big Model |\n|----------------|------------|-----------|\n| Dropout rate (Pdrop) | 0.1 [2] | 0.1 [1] |\n| Checkpoint averaging | Last 5 checkpoints [1] | Last 20 checkpoints [1] |\n| Beam size | 4 [1] | 4 [1] |\n| Length penalty (α) | 0.6 [1] | 0.6 [1] |\n| Maximum output length | Input length + 50 [1] | Input length + 50 [1] |\n| Label smoothing (ϵls) | 0.1 [2] | Not specified in fragments |\n| Warmup steps | 4000 [2] | Not specified in fragments |\n\n**Additional notes:**\n- The big model used a dropout rate of 0.1 instead of 0.3 [1].\n- Checkpoints were written at 10-minute intervals for both models [1].\n- These hyperparameters were chosen after experimentation on the development set [1].\n- The document mentions that for the big model, \"unlisted values are identical to those of the base model\" in the context of model variations [3], but specific architecture dimensions (like layers, hidden size, etc.) for the big model are not fully detailed in the provided fragments.",

backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3427:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are the key financial metrics for NVIDIA in fiscal year 2024 (ended January 28, 2024):\n\n- **Revenue**: $60.9 billion, up 126% from the prior year [2]. The exact figure is **$60,922 million** [1][2].\n- **Net Income**: **$29,760 million**, up 581% from the prior year [2][8].\n- **Gross Margin**: **72.7%**, up 15.8 percentage points from 56.9% in the prior year [1][2].\n\nAdditional details:\n- **Operating Income**: $32,972 million, up 681% [1][2].\n- **Net Income per Diluted Share**: $11.93, up 586% [2][8].\n- **Operating Expenses**: $11,329 million, up 2% [2][8].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3464:      "response_text": "I can only answer questions about the provided document(s). Would you like to ask about its content?\n\nThe document fragments I have are about NVIDIA Corporation's Compensation Recovery Policy (also known as a clawback policy), including details about:\n\n- **Covered Officers** and their obligations under the policy [1]\n- **Recoverable Incentive Compensation** and how it's calculated [6]\n- **Executive Acknowledgment** forms that officers must sign [2][3][6]\n- **Committee administration** and indemnification of Board members [8]\n- References to **Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 304** and **Nasdaq Listing Rule 5608** [1][6]\n- Various **risk factors** related to NVIDIA's business [4][7]\n\nIs there anything specific about these documents you'd like me to help with?",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3506:      "response_text": "I can only answer questions about the provided document(s). Would you like to ask about its content?\n\nBased on the document fragments, I cannot confirm that finding — in fact, the paper states the **opposite**. The Transformer is presented as a **superior alternative** to recurrent neural networks (RNNs):\n\n- The paper proposes the Transformer as a new architecture that **dispenses with recurrence entirely**, relying solely on attention mechanisms [1].\n- It explicitly states that the Transformer is **\"superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train\"** compared to recurrent or convolutional models [1].\n- The Transformer achieves **state-of-the-art results** on WMT 2014 English-to-German (28.4 BLEU) and English-to-French (41.8 BLEU) translation tasks, outperforming existing best results including ensembles [1][5].\n- The conclusion describes the Transformer as **\"the first sequence transduction model based entirely on attention, replacing the recurrent layers most commonly used in encoder-decoder architectures\"** [3].\n- It also notes that the Transformer **\"can be trained significantly faster than architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers\"** [3].\n\nThe paper does acknowledge that RNNs (including LSTMs and GRUs) were previously the state-of-the-art for sequence modeling [2], but it positions the Transformer as an improvement over them, not as inferior.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3546:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, NVIDIA's revenue **did not decline** in fiscal year 2024. In fact, the company experienced significant growth.\n\nHere are the relevant details from the documents:\n\n- **Revenue increased by 126%**: Total revenue for fiscal year 2024 was **$60.9 billion**, up 126% from the prior year [2].\n- **Specific figures**: Revenue grew from $26,974 million in fiscal year 2023 to $60,922 million in fiscal year 2024 [1][2].\n- **Segment growth**: The Compute & Networking segment revenue increased by 215%, and the Graphics segment increased by 14% [1].\n- **Data Center performance**: Data Center revenue was up 217%, with Data Center compute revenue specifically up 244% [2].\n\nThe documents do not mention any 50% revenue decline. Instead, they describe strong demand driven by enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and multiple industry verticals including automotive, financial services, and healthcare [2].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3581:        "fragments"
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3620:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are all the mentioned advantages of the Transformer model:\n\n*   **Superior Quality**: The model is described as being \"superior in quality\" [1].\n*   **More Parallelizable**: The architecture is \"more parallelizable\" compared to recurrent models, which have an inherently sequential nature that precludes parallelization within training examples [1][2].\n*   **Significantly Less Time to Train**: The Transformer requires \"significantly less time to train\" [1]. This is supported by Table 2, which shows the model achieving better BLEU scores \"at a fraction of the training cost\" compared to other state-of-the-art models [6].\n*   **State-of-the-Art Results**: The model achieves new state-of-the-art results on specific translation tasks. It achieved 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over existing best results by over 2 BLEU, and a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8 on the English-to-French translation task [1].\n*   **Generalization to Other Tasks**: The Transformer can generalize to other tasks beyond translation, performing \"surprisingly well\" on English constituency parsing and yielding better results than all previously reported models except one [5].\n*   **Advantages of Self-Attention**: The paper motivates self-attention and discusses its advantages over recurrent and convolutional models, though the specific details of these advantages are not fully elaborated in the provided fragments [3].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3660:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, NVIDIA mentions the following product categories and platforms:\n\n- **Data Center accelerated computing platforms** [1]\n- **End-to-end networking platforms** (including Quantum for InfiniBand and Spectrum for Ethernet) [1]\n- **NVIDIA DRIVE automated-driving platform** [1]\n- **Gaming platforms** (including GeForce RTX and GeForce GTX GPUs for desktop and laptop PCs, GeForce NOW cloud gaming, and SoCs) [6]\n- **Professional Visualization** [8]\n- **Automotive** [8]\n- **DGX Cloud** (AI-training-as-a-service platform) [3]\n- **NeMo** (a complete solution for building enterprise-ready Large Language Models) [3]\n- **NVIDIA AI Enterprise software** [5]\n- **Networking offerings** (end-to-end platforms for InfiniBand and Ethernet, consisting of network adapters, cables, DPUs, and switch systems, plus a full software stack) [5]\n- **NVIDIA RTX** (featuring ray tracing technology and DLSS) [6]\n\nThe document also states that NVIDIA's platforms address four large markets: **Data Center, Gaming, Professional Visualization, and Automotive** [8].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/official_deepseek_v4_full_2026-05-05.json:3700:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are the exceptions and carve-outs mentioned in the agreement:\n\n- **Disclosure to employees and professional advisers**: The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to its employees and professional advisers, but only under specific conditions. These individuals must need to know the information for the Purpose, know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party, and be bound by obligations consistent with the agreement [2].\n- **Written agreement of the disclosing party**: The Recipient may use the Confidential Information for a purpose other than the Purpose if it first obtains the written agreement of the other party [2].\n\nNo other exceptions or carve-outs are mentioned in the provided fragments.",

backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:82:      "response_text": "Based on the document fragments, NVIDIA's total revenue for fiscal year 2024 was **$60,922 million** (or $60.9 billion) [1][2][3].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:111:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments:\n\n**Data Center segment revenue for fiscal year 2024** was **$47,525 million** (or $47.5 billion) [4].\n\n**Gaming segment revenue for fiscal year 2024** was **$10,447 million** (or $10.4 billion) [4][6].\n\n**Comparison:**\n* The Data Center segment revenue was significantly larger, accounting for the vast majority of the company's total revenue of $60.9 billion [1][4].\n* The Gaming segment revenue was less than a quarter of the Data Center segment's revenue.\n* Year-over-year growth for Data Center revenue was up 217% [1], while Gaming revenue was up 15% from the previous fiscal year [6].\n\n**Summary Table:**\n\n| Segment | FY 2024 Revenue | Year-over-Year Change |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Data Center** | $47,525 million | Up 217% [1] |\n| **Gaming** | $10,447 million | Up 15% [6] |\n\n**Additional Context:**\n* The Compute & Networking reportable segment, which includes Data Center revenue, had total revenue of $47,405 million for FY 2024 [2]. The slight difference between this figure and the standalone Data Center revenue ($47,525 million) is likely due to the inclusion of other networking-related revenues in the Compute & Networking segment total.\n* Strong Data Center demand was driven by enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and multiple industry verticals including automotive, financial services, and healthcare [1].\n* In the fourth quarter of FY 2024, large cloud providers represented more than half of the Data Center revenue [6].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:142:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the authors argue that self-attention is better than recurrence for long sequences primarily due to **parallelization** and **computational complexity**.\n\nHere are the key points from the fragments:\n\n*   **Parallelization Limitation of Recurrent Models:** Recurrent models (like RNNs, LSTMs) process sequences step-by-step, where the computation for position `t` depends on the hidden state from position `t-1`. This creates an \"inherently sequential nature\" that \"precludes parallelization within training examples.\" The authors state this becomes \"critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples\" [2].\n*   **Computational Complexity as a Desideratum:** When comparing self-attention to recurrent and convolutional layers, one of the three key considerations (\"desiderata\") is \"the total computational complexity per layer.\" Another is \"the amount of computation that can be parallelized\" [1].\n*   **Advantage of Self-Attention:** The proposed Transformer architecture is \"based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.\" A stated result is that these models are \"superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train\" [8]. This implies that by removing the sequential dependency of recurrence, self-attention overcomes the parallelization bottleneck for long sequences.\n\nIn summary, the authors' argument centers on the **sequential dependency of recurrent models**, which prevents effective parallelization and becomes a major constraint for long sequences. **Self-attention**, by contrast, allows for greater parallelization, leading to reduced training times and making it more suitable for handling long sequences [1][2][8].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:172:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, **no specific penalty amount for breaching the NDA is mentioned** [1][2].\n\nThe fragments outline obligations and general terms, such as:\n* The recipient's undertaking not to use confidential information except for the stated Purpose [1].\n* The obligation to keep information secure and limit disclosure [1].\n* The duration of confidentiality obligations [2].\n* Governing law and jurisdiction clauses [2].\n\nHowever, the sections provided do not include any clauses specifying monetary penalties, liquidated damages, or other financial consequences for a breach.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:230:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is NVIDIA's segment revenue breakdown in table format:\n\n| Reportable Segment | Year Ended Jan 28, 2024 ($ in millions) | Year Ended Jan 29, 2023 ($ in millions) | Year-on-Year $ Change | Year-on-Year % Change |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Compute & Networking** | $47,405 | $15,068 | +$32,337 | +215% |\n| **Graphics** | $13,517 | $11,906 | +$1,611 | +14% |\n| **Total Revenue** | **$60,922** | **$26,974** | **+$33,948** | **+126%** |\n\n**Sources and Additional Context:**\n* The revenue figures and percentage changes for the reportable segments are explicitly provided in the document [1].\n* The \"All Other\" category is mentioned in the context of operating income, not revenue, and is described as containing expenses not assigned to the two main segments for performance assessment [4]. Therefore, it is not included as a revenue-generating segment in this breakdown.\n* The total revenue for fiscal years 2024 and 2023 is confirmed in multiple fragments [1][3][6].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:286:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here are all the exceptions and carve-outs mentioned in the agreement:\n\n*   **Exception to the purpose restriction:** The Recipient may use the Confidential Information for a purpose other than the agreed Purpose, but only if they first obtain the **written agreement of the other party**[2].\n*   **Exception to the non-disclosure obligation:** The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to **its employees [and professional advisers]** who meet the following conditions[2]:\n    *   They **need to know the information for the Purpose**.\n    *   They **know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party**.\n    *   They **are bound by obligations** (presumably of confidentiality, though the fragment cuts off)[2].\n*   **Carve-out from confidentiality obligations:** The undertakings in the agreement (clauses 3 and 4, covering use and disclosure) **do not apply to information that the Recipient can show**[1]:\n    *   Was in the **public domain** at the time of disclosure, unless due to a breach of this agreement.\n    *   Was already in its **possession** before disclosure.\n    *   Was **lawfully obtained** from a third party without obligations of confidentiality.\n    *   Is **independently developed** without using the Confidential Information.\n    *   Must be disclosed due to a **legal obligation, court order, or government authority**, provided the disclosing party is given advance notice to contest the disclosure[1].\n\n**Citation:** [1][2]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:367:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is a comparison of NVIDIA's Data Center and Gaming segment revenue for fiscal year 2024:\n\n- **Data Center Revenue:** $47,525 million (up 217% from fiscal year 2023) [4][1]\n- **Gaming Revenue:** $10,447 million (up 15% from fiscal year 2023) [4][6]\n\n**Key Comparison:**\n- **Data Center** revenue was significantly larger, accounting for **$47.5 billion** compared to Gaming's **$10.4 billion**.\n- **Data Center** revenue grew at a much faster rate (**217%** year-over-year) versus Gaming's **15%** growth.\n- Data Center revenue was more than **4.5 times** the size of Gaming revenue in fiscal year 2024.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:397:      "response_text": "The authors argue that self-attention is better than recurrence for long sequences primarily due to **parallelization** and **computational efficiency**:\n\n- **Sequential nature of recurrence limits parallelization**: Recurrent models compute hidden states sequentially along symbol positions, which \"precludes parallelization within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples\" [2].\n- **Self-attention enables parallel computation**: Unlike recurrence, self-attention relates different positions of a single sequence without requiring sequence-aligned steps, allowing the model to process all positions simultaneously [7].\n- **Three desiderata motivate self-attention**: The authors compare self-attention to recurrent and convolutional layers based on (1) total computational complexity per layer, (2) amount of computation that can be parallelized, and (3) a third factor (the fragment cuts off) [1]. The parallelization advantage is especially important for longer sequences.\n\nIn summary, **self-attention avoids the inherent sequential bottleneck of recurrence**, making it more scalable and efficient for long sequences.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:427:      "response_text": "This information is not present in the provided document. The fragments outline the obligations of confidentiality, the purpose of disclosure, and the governing law, but they do **not** specify any penalty amount or financial consequence for breaching the NDA.",

backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:731:      "response_text": "Here is NVIDIA's segment revenue breakdown presented in a table format based on the provided document fragments:\n\n| **Segment**            | **Year Ended Jan 28, 2024 ($ in millions)** | **Year Ended Jan 29, 2023 ($ in millions)** | **Change ($ in millions)** | **Change (%)** |\n|-------------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------|----------------|\n| Compute & Networking     | 47,405                                      | 15,068                                      | 32,337                     | 215%           |\n| Graphics                | 60,922                                      | 26,974                                      | 33,948                     | 126%           |\n| **Total**               | **108,327**                                 | **42,042**                                  | **66,285**                 | **158%**       |\n\n*Note: The \"Total\" row sums the two segments, though the document also mentions \"All Other\" with negative values in operating income context [1][4]. However, revenue by segment explicitly lists only Compute & Networking and Graphics [1][3][6].*  \n\nSources: [1][3][6]",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:869:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, here is the comparison of NVIDIA's Data Center and Gaming segment revenues for fiscal year 2024:\n\n* **Data Center Revenue:** $47.525 billion ($47,525 million) [4]. Together, these two specialized markets accounted for the vast majority of NVIDIA's total FY2024 revenue of $60.9 billion [1][4].\n* **Growth Drivers:** The explosive growth in Data Center revenue was primarily driven by strong demand for AI infrastructure across enterprise software, consumer internet applications, and various industry verticals [1]. Gaming revenue growth was supported by the launch of new GeForce RTX 40-series GPUs and the expansion of AI-powered gaming technologies like DLSS and ray tracing [6].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:899:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the authors argue that self-attention is superior to recurrent architectures for long sequences primarily due to **parallelization capabilities and computational efficiency**:\n\n* **Sequential Bottleneck in Recurrence:** Recurrent models (e.g., LSTMs, GRUs) process sequences step-by-step, where each hidden state depends on the previous one. This \"inherently sequential nature precludes parallelization within training examples\" [2].\n* **Critical Scaling Issues for Long Sequences:** The lack of parallelization becomes a major bottleneck specifically for longer sequences, as \"memory constraints limit batching across examples\" during training [2].\n* **Self-Attention Enables Parallelization:** By dispensing with recurrence entirely and relying solely on self-attention, the Transformer architecture allows for much greater parallelization. The authors note that this makes their model \"more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train\" [8].\n* **Key Design Motivations:** When comparing self-attention to recurrent and convolutional layers, the authors explicitly cite \"total computational complexity per layer\" and \"the amount of computation that can be parallelized\" as core desiderata driving their architectural choice [1].\n\nIn summary, the authors favor self-attention because it eliminates the sequential processing bottleneck of RNNs, enabling efficient parallel computation that scales much better with increasing sequence lengths.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:929:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, **this information is not present in the provided document.** The excerpts only cover the agreement's purpose, confidentiality obligations, the duration of the undertakings, governing law/jurisdiction, and signature blocks, with no mention of financial penalties, liquidated damages, or other breach remedies.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1039:      "response_text": "Based on the provided document fragments, the following exceptions and carve-outs are explicitly mentioned:\n\n* **Copying Exception:** The right to copy the Confidential Information is restricted solely to the Purpose [1].\n* **Use Exception:** The Recipient may not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Purpose, unless it first obtains the written agreement of the other party [2].\n* **Disclosure Exception (Employees & Professional Advisers):** The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to third parties only under the following specific carve-outs:\n  * Limited to its **employees** and **professional advisers** [2].\n  * Only if they **need to know** the information for the Purpose [2].\n  * Only if they **know they owe a duty of confidence** to the other party [2].\n  * Only if they are **bound by corresponding obligations** (note: the text cuts off here, but implies confidentiality/non-disclosure obligations) [2].\n\n*Note: The provided fragments are excerpted from a confidentiality agreement, and some clause continuations are truncated. The exceptions listed above reflect all carve-outs explicitly visible in the provided text.*",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1066:      "response_text": "I can only answer questions about the provided document(s). Would you like to ask about its content? I can help you analyze the confidentiality obligations, governing law, duration, or party details outlined in the fragments.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1151:      "response_text": "## Why Self-Attention Outperforms Recurrence for Long Sequences\n\nBased on the provided document fragments, the authors present the following key arguments:\n\n### 1. **Parallelization Limitations of Recurrent Models**\nThe authors explain that recurrent models \"typically factor computation along the symbol positions of the input and output sequences\" [2]. They generate hidden states as a function of the previous hidden state and the current input, creating an \"inherently sequential nature\" that \"precludes parallelization within training examples\" [2].\n\n### 2. **Memory Constraints at Long Sequences**\nThis sequential computation becomes \"critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples\" [2]. The inability to parallelize means longer sequences are more difficult to handle efficiently.\n\n### 3. **Computational Complexity Considerations**\nThe authors mention they compare self-attention layers against recurrent and convolutional layers based on:\n- Total computational complexity per layer\n- Amount of computation that can be parallelized [1]\n\n### 4. **Relative Position Learning**\nThe sinusoidal positional encoding allows the model to \"easily learn to attend by relative positions\" since for any fixed offset k, PE(pos+k) can be represented as a linear function of PE(pos) [1]. This may also allow the model to \"extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training\" [1].\n\n### 5. **First Pure Attention Model**\nThe Transformer is described as \"the first transduction model relying entirely on self-attention to compute representations of its input and output without using sequence aligned RNNs or convolution\" [7].\n\n---\n\n**In summary:** The main advantage is that self-attention enables **parallel computation** across sequence positions, overcoming the sequential bottleneck of recurrence that limits efficiency and scalability for long sequences.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1181:      "response_text": "The penalty amount for breaching this NDA is **not specified** in the provided document fragments.\n\nThe document outlines the **obligations** of the Recipient (e.g., keeping information secure, not disclosing to third parties, using information only for the stated Purpose) [1], as well as the **duration** of these undertakings and the **governing law** (English law) [2]. However, no specific monetary penalty or damages amount for breach is mentioned in either fragment.\n\nYou may wish to:\n- Review the complete NDA document for a liquidated damages clause\n- Consult a legal professional regarding standard breach remedies under English law\n\nIf you have access to additional pages of this Agreement, the penalty provision may be included elsewhere in the document.",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1239:      "response_text": "## NVIDIA Segment Revenue Breakdown\n\nBased on the provided document fragments, here is the segment revenue breakdown:\n\n| **Reportable Segment** | **FY 2024 (Jan 28, 2024)** | **FY 2023 (Jan 29, 2023)** | **Change ($)** | **Change (%)** |\n|------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------------|----------------|\n| Compute & Networking   | $47,405 million            | $15,068 million            | $32,337 million| 215%           |\n| Graphics               | $13,517 million           | $11,906 million           | $1,611 million | 14%            |\n| **Total**              | **$60,922 million**        | **$26,974 million**        | **$33,948 million** | **126%**   |\n\n**Source:** Form 10-K for fiscal year ended January 29, 2023 [1]\n\n---\n\n### Key Observations\n\n- **Compute & Networking** segment experienced dramatic growth (215%), driven primarily by Data Center revenue which was up 217% in fiscal year 2024 [1][6]\n- **Graphics** segment showed more modest growth (14%) compared to the prior year [1]\n- **Total revenue** increased by 126%, nearly tripling year-over-year [1]\n\nAdditionally, the document notes that revenue from sales to customers outside of the United States accounted for 56%, 69%, and 84% of total revenue for fiscal years 2024, 2023, and 2022 respectively [3].",
backend/scripts/benchmark_results/china_models_shortlist_prod_smoke_2026-05-05.json:1294:      "response_text": "## Exceptions and Carve-outs in the Agreement\n\nBased on the provided document fragments, the following exceptions and carve-outs are mentioned:\n\n### 1. **Right to Copy**\nThe agreement carves out \"the right to copy the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party solely for the Purpose\" [2]\n\n### 2. **Disclosure to Employees and Professional Advisers**\nClause 4 contains an exception allowing disclosure to third parties under specific conditions:\n\n- **Employees** (who need to know for the Purpose)\n- **Professional advisers** (optional inclusion — shown in brackets)\n- **Conditions** for this exception:\n  - They must need to know the information for the Purpose\n  - They must know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party\n  - They must be bound by obligations [2]\n\n### 3. **Duration Options**\nThe undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 can continue either:\n- **Indefinitely**, OR\n- **For a specified number of years** from the date of the Agreement (placeholder: \"[insert number]\") [1]\n\n### 4. **Governing Jurisdiction (Minor Carve-out)**\nThe agreement specifies \"non-exclusive jurisdiction\" for disputes, meaning parties are not restricted exclusively to the English Courts — they could also pursue litigation elsewhere [1]\n\n---\n\n> **Note:** This agreement contains multiple placeholder brackets (e.g., `[insert details]`, `[NAME OF COMPANY]`, `[number on Register of Companies]`), indicating it is a template requiring customization before execution. Additional exceptions may appear in portions not included in the provided fragments.",

/bin/zsh -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
from app.services.query_router import query_router
cases=[
\"top 5 reasons in this doc\",
\"what is p value in table 2?\",
\"pages 5 and 6 compare revenue\",
\"show table on page 8\",
\"summarize page 5\",
\"which pages mention margin\",
\"str. 5 revenue\",
\"p. 5 revenue\",
\"pageRank algorithm overview\",
]
for q in cases:
    r=query_router.route(q)
    print(q,'=>',r.primary_intent,r.page_ref,r.intents,r.reason)
PY" in /Users/mayijie/Projects/Code/010_DocTalk/backend

 def _matches_any(text: str, patterns: tuple[str, ...]) -> bool:
     return any(re.search(pattern, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE) for pattern in patterns)
 
@@ -171,6 +207,22 @@ class QueryRouter:
         has_exhaustive = _matches_any(normalized, _EXHAUSTIVE_MARKERS)
         has_citation = _matches_any(normalized, _CITATION_MARKERS)
 
+        # Positional lookup takes precedence: a specific page number can't be
+        # resolved by semantic top-k, so route straight to a direct page fetch.
+        # (Skip when it's a summary/exhaustive request like "summarize page 5".)
+        page_ref = _detect_page_ref(normalized)
+        if page_ref is not None and not has_summary and not has_exhaustive:
+            return QueryRoute(
+                primary_intent=QueryIntent.PAGE_LOOKUP,
+                intents=(QueryIntent.PAGE_LOOKUP,),
+                scope=_route_scope(is_collection=is_collection),
+                coverage="section",
+                confidence=0.9,
+                reason="specific page reference",
+                rewritten_queries=(normalized,),
+                page_ref=page_ref,
+            )
+
         if has_summary and has_whole_doc and not has_section:
             intents.append(QueryIntent.DOCUMENT_SUMMARY)
             coverage = "whole_doc"

     1	"""Generate persisted hierarchical document briefs after parsing.
     2	
     3	Runs in Celery worker context with synchronous DB and OpenAI-compatible clients.
     4	This is best-effort product metadata generation: failures are persisted on the
     5	brief row when possible, but never change the document's parse status.
     6	"""
     7	from __future__ import annotations
     8	
     9	import json
    10	import re
    11	import uuid
    12	from datetime import datetime, timezone

    16	from openai import OpenAI

    25	logger = get_task_logger(__name__)

    27	BRIEF_PROMPT_VERSION = "document_brief_v1"
    28	BRIEF_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1
    29	BRIEF_MODEL = settings.MODE_MODELS.get("quick", settings.LLM_MODEL)
    30	BRIEF_MAX_CHUNKS = 24
    31	BRIEF_MAX_CHARS_PER_CHUNK = 1000
    32	
    33	BRIEF_PROMPT = """You are building a structured document brief for a professional document workspace.
    34	
    35	Use only the numbered document fragments. Every outline item, key point, and fact must cite one or more fragment numbers from the provided context.
    36	
    37	Return valid JSON only:
    38	{{
    39	  "summary": "One concise paragraph explaining what the document is and why it matters.",
    40	  "outline": [
    41	    {{"title": "Section or theme", "level": 1, "summary": "What this section/theme covers.", "source_refs": [1]}}
    42	  ],
    43	  "key_points": [
    44	    {{"text": "Important takeaway stated as a complete sentence.", "source_refs": [1, 2]}}
    45	  ],
    46	  "facts": [
    47	    {{"label": "Metric, date, party, clause, or finding", "value": "Exact value or short answer", "context": "Why it matters.", "source_refs": [3]}}
    48	  ],
    49	  "questions": [
    50	    "A useful question a reader may ask next?"
    51	  ]
    52	}}
    53	
    54	Caps:
    55	- summary: 1 paragraph.
    56	- outline: 4 to 8 items.
    57	- key_points: 5 to 10 items.
    58	- facts: up to 12 items.
    59	- questions: exactly 5 concise questions.
    60	
    61	Document fragments:
    62	---
    63	{chunks_text}
    64	---
    65	"""
    66	
    67	
    68	def _is_deepseek_official_model(model: str) -> bool:
    69	    return model in settings.DEEPSEEK_OFFICIAL_MODELS
    70	
    71	
    72	def _get_llm_client(model: str) -> OpenAI | None:
    73	    if _is_deepseek_official_model(model):
    74	        if not settings.DEEPSEEK_API_KEY:
    75	            return None
    76	        return OpenAI(api_key=settings.DEEPSEEK_API_KEY, base_url=settings.DEEPSEEK_BASE_URL)
    77	    if not settings.OPENROUTER_API_KEY:
    78	        return None
    79	    return OpenAI(api_key=settings.OPENROUTER_API_KEY, base_url=settings.OPENROUTER_BASE_URL)
    80	
    81	
    82	def _apply_provider_options(kwargs: dict[str, Any], model: str) -> None:
    83	    if _is_deepseek_official_model(model):
    84	        kwargs["extra_body"] = {"thinking": {"type": "disabled"}}
    85	
    86	
    87	def _json_from_text(text: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    88	    content = (text or "").strip()
    89	    if content.startswith("```"):
    90	        content = re.sub(r"^```(?:json)?\s*", "", content)
    91	        content = re.sub(r"\s*```$", "", content)
    92	    try:
    93	        data = json.loads(content)
    94	    except json.JSONDecodeError:
    95	        match = re.search(r"\{.*\}", content, flags=re.DOTALL)
    96	        if not match:
    97	            raise
    98	        data = json.loads(match.group(0))
    99	    if not isinstance(data, dict):
   100	        raise ValueError("Brief response must be a JSON object")
   101	    return data

   103	
   104	def _str(value: Any, fallback: str = "", *, limit: int = 4000) -> str:
   105	    text = str(value).strip() if value is not None else fallback

   151	
   152	    exact_terms = _dedupe_preserve_order(exact_candidates, limit=limit)
   153	    lexical_terms = _dedupe_preserve_order([*exact_terms, *lexical_candidates], limit=limit)
   154	    return QueryEvidenceTerms(lexical_terms=lexical_terms, exact_terms=exact_terms)
   155	
   156	
   157	def _result_text(result: dict) -> str:
   158	    return " ".join(
   159	        str(part or "")
   160	        for part in (
   161	            result.get("section_title"),
   162	            result.get("text"),
   163	            result.get("document_filename"),
   164	        )
   165	    ).lower()
   166	
   167	
   168	def _matched_terms(terms: tuple[str, ...], results: list[dict]) -> tuple[str, ...]:
   169	    if not terms or not results:
   170	        return ()
   171	    haystack = "\n".join(_result_text(result) for result in results)
   172	    return tuple(term for term in terms if term.lower() in haystack)
   173	
   174	
   175	def _prompt_note(status: RetrievalStatus, reason: str, *, corrected: bool, route: QueryRoute) -> str:
   176	    coverage_note = ""
   177	    if route.coverage == "exhaustive_scan":
   178	        coverage_note = (
   179	            "The user asked for broad or existence-style coverage. Treat the fragments as the searched evidence set, "
   180	            "state limits clearly, and do not infer beyond cited text."
   181	        )
   182	    elif QueryIntent.TABLE_QUERY in route.intents:
   183	        coverage_note = (
   184	            "The user is asking about tables, figures, or metrics; prioritize structured table evidence, preserve row labels, "
   185	            "units, periods, and currencies exactly, and cite the supporting fragment for each numeric claim."
   186	        )
   187	    elif QueryIntent.CITATION_LOOKUP in route.intents:
   188	        coverage_note = "The user is asking for source location or quotation; prioritize exact cited evidence over broad explanation."
   189	
   190	    if status == "empty":
   191	        base = "No relevant document fragments were found for this query. Say that the document evidence was not found instead of guessing."
   192	    elif status == "weak":
   193	        base = "Retrieved evidence may be incomplete or weak. Answer only what the cited fragments support and call out missing evidence."
   194	    else:
   195	        base = "Retrieved evidence appears adequate. Keep every factual claim tied to the cited fragments."
   196	
   197	    correction_note = " Corrective lexical retrieval was applied." if corrected else ""
   198	    return " ".join(part for part in (base, coverage_note, correction_note) if part).strip()
   199	
   200	
   201	class RAGEvaluatorService:
   202	    def evaluate(
   203	        self,
   204	        query: str,
   205	        results: list[dict],
   206	        route: QueryRoute,
   207	        *,
   208	        corrected: bool = False,
   209	    ) -> RetrievalEvaluation:
   210	        terms = extract_query_terms(query)
   211	        query_terms = terms.lexical_terms
   212	        exact_terms = terms.exact_terms
   213	        matched = _matched_terms(query_terms, results)
   214	        missing = tuple(term for term in query_terms if term not in matched)
   215	        matched_exact = _matched_terms(exact_terms, results)
   216	        best_score = max((float(result.get("score") or 0.0) for result in results), default=0.0)
   217	
   218	        if not results:
   219	            status: RetrievalStatus = "empty"
   220	            reason = "no_retrieved_fragments"
   221	        elif exact_terms and len(matched_exact) < len(exact_terms):
   222	            status = "weak"
   223	            reason = "exact_terms_missing"
   224	        elif route.coverage == "exhaustive_scan" and len(results) < 6:
   225	            status = "weak"
   226	            reason = "exhaustive_query_undercovered"
   227	        elif query_terms and len(matched) == 0 and best_score < 0.45:
   228	            status = "weak"
   229	            reason = "low_score_no_term_overlap"
   230	        elif best_score < 0.22:
   231	            status = "weak"
   232	            reason = "low_vector_score"
   233	        else:
   234	            status = "sufficient"
   235	            reason = "evidence_sufficient"
   236	
   237	        should_correct = status in {"empty", "weak"} or route.coverage == "exhaustive_scan" or any(
   238	            intent in route.intents
   239	            for intent in (QueryIntent.TABLE_QUERY, QueryIntent.CITATION_LOOKUP, QueryIntent.EXISTENCE_CHECK)
   240	        )
